coredata.dc:title: Tribochemistry of cyclohexanol between self-mated YG8 interfaces and its friction-reduction mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Renhui
Wang, Qing
Zhang, Qiao
Fu, Xiuwen
Luo, Yuzhou
Tang, Gang
He, Zhongyi
coredata.dc:description: Water-solubility alcohols are reported to exhibit excellent lubricity at both low and high normal loads during sliding, and the lubrication mechanisms for water-solubility alcohols are well known until now. However, the lubricity of water-insolubility alcohols is rarely reported and its lubrication mechanisms were still not well understood so far. Here, we first investigate the lubricity of cyclohexanol between self-mated YG8 balls. The experimental and computational results reveal that cyclohexene and its derivative, and tungsten oxide is initially indeed formed during sliding, then, the polymerization is induced by friction-induced effect between cyclohexene and its derivative, revealing that the formation of polymers can effectively stabilize friction coefficient, and the formation of tungsten oxide plays an important role in enhancing wear resistance. Besides, for 196 N, the formation of carbon quantum dots makes friction coefficient reduce with increasing sliding time compared to that of 98 N. The new find will be potentially applied in machinery and equipment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tribochemistry
Cyclohexanol
YG8
Normal load

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Raman spectra of lubrication liquid before and after frictional tests
The microstructure of transferred materials on wear scars

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 51605336 ) and Jiangxi Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 20202BAB204020 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The curves of normal load, friction moment and friction coefficient with respect to sliding time under 98 N and 1450 rpm.
The curves of normal load, friction moment and friction coefficient with respect to sliding time under 196 N and 1450 rpm.
The wear scars of tribosystems sliding in cyclohexanol under (a) 98 N and (b) 196 N. Note: the average wear scar diameters are about (a) 316.06 and (b) 412.52 µm.
(a) SEM image of wear scar under 98 N and corresponding EDS mappings: (b) C and (c) O.
(a) SEM image of wear scar under 196 N and corresponding EDS mappings: (b) C and (c) O.
Raman spectra of lubrication liquid before (cyclohexanol) and after frictional tests.
The transition states search results for the catalytic transformation from cyclohexanol to cyclohexene.
The transition states search results for the catalytic transformation from cyclohexanol to cyclohexanone.
XPS spectra of transferred materials on wear scar sliding in cyclohexanol under 98 N: (a) C 1 s, (b) O 1 s, (c) Co 2p and (d) W 4 f.
XPS spectra of transferred materials on wear scar sliding in cyclohexanol under 196 N: (a) C 1 s, (b) O 1 s, (c) Co 2p and (d) W 4 f.

(a) TEM image of nanotribofilm under 98 N sliding in cyclohexanol using FIB technique. (b) Enlarged TEM image of the red square marked region “A” in a. (c) High resolution TEM image of the red square marked region in b. (d) Enlarged TEM image of the red square marked region “B” in a. (e) High resolution TEM image of the red square marked region in d.

(a) TEM image of nanotribofilm under 196 N sliding in cyclohexanol using FIB technique. (b) Enlarged TEM image of red rectangle marked region in a. (c) High resolution TEM image of red square marked region “A” in b. (d) High resolution TEM image of red square marked region “B” in b.
Schematic depict of the lubrication mechanism.

head.ce:title: Tribochemistry of cyclohexanol between self-mated YG8 interfaces and its friction-reduction mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, East China JiaoTong University, Nanchang 330013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Architecture and Civil Engineering, Anhui University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Architecture and Civil Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Ma’anshan, Anhui, 243002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water-solubility alcohols are reported to exhibit excellent lubricity at both low and high normal loads during sliding, and the lubrication mechanisms for water-solubility alcohols are well known until now. However, the lubricity of water-insolubility alcohols is rarely reported and its lubrication mechanisms were still not well understood so far. Here, we first investigate the lubricity of cyclohexanol between self-mated YG8 balls. The experimental and computational results reveal that cyclohexene and its derivative, and tungsten oxide is initially indeed formed during sliding, then, the polymerization is induced by friction-induced effect between cyclohexene and its derivative, revealing that the formation of polymers can effectively stabilize friction coefficient, and the formation of tungsten oxide plays an important role in enhancing wear resistance. Besides, for 196 N, the formation of carbon quantum dots makes friction coefficient reduce with increasing sliding time compared to that of 98 N. The new find will be potentially applied in machinery and equipment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Tribochemical reactions occurred between the tribopair's interfaces closely affect the tribological properties of the tribosystems, which were of importance to solve tribological problems [1,2]. The tribosystem, the friction conditions and the time were treated as the major factors for tribochemical reactions [3]. Among them, the friction-induced conditions such as normal loads, sliding speed, and tribopairs with catalytic activity were of importance, they were effectively controlled leading to effectively influence the tribological performances.

Thus, many researchers focused on these three factors to achieve the effective lubrication of the tribosystem. Superlubricity at the macroscale was observed as sliding in several liquid lubricants, for instance, Matta and coworkers reported the superlubricity of glycerol at the normal load and sliding speed of 3 N and 0.3 m/s under the self-mated ta-C films, and they believed that the OH-terminated surfaces induced by tribochemical reactions that easily glided conducted the superlubricity [4]. Luo et al. reported the superlubricity of sulfuric acid solution mixed with alcohols solutions between glass plate and Si3N4 interfaces at the normal load and sliding speed of 3 N and 0.075 m/s [5,6]. Besides, they investigated the tribological properties of self-mated AISI 52100 steel at 3 N and 0.15 m/s as sliding in glycerol with variable humidity, the controllable superlubricity was achieved [7]. The hydrated layer induced by tribochemical reactions could be in charge of superlubricity [8], besides, Zhou et al [,9]. systematically illustrated and summarized the proposed liquid superlubricity mechanisms, smooth contacting interfaces, low viscosities as well as minimum shear resistances were the three important factors for superlubricity. However, one may wonder, how about the stability of hydrated layers induced by tribochemical reactions under high normal loads and fast sliding speed?

In fact, the hydrated layers were unstable under high normal loads and fast sliding speed, because they were easily damaged when increasing the loads and speed, as reported by Montgomery [10] and Kajdas [11]. Besides, Zhang et al [17]. investigated the tribological behaviors of water-soluble polyols (ethanediol, 1,3-butanediol, propanetriol and 1,4-butanediol), they pointed out that the number and location of hydroxyl in polyols played an important role in modulating their tribological behaviors. Thus, in our previous work, we have systematically investigated the lubricity of water-solubility alcohols such as methanol, ethylene glycol, isopropanol and glycerol under high normal loads (98 or 196 N) and 1450 rpm, we reported that the amorphous carbon or graphene induced by tribochemical reactions was in the charge of low friction under high normal loads (98 or 196 N) and 1450 rpm as sliding in alcohols [12–16]. However, there were rarely reports about the lubricity of water-insoluble alcohols.

Thus, the lubricity of water-insoluble alcohols was worth further being investigated. Cyclohexanol with six-membered ring was one of water-insolubility alcohols, how about its lubricity? It was worth deeply investigating. Thus, in this work, we focus on investigating the lubricity of cyclohexanol between self-mated YG8 balls. The good tribological properties were found as sliding in cyclohexanol between self-mated YG8 balls under 98, 196 N and 1450 rpm. And the proposed tribological mechanisms were investigated in detail.

The cross-sectional slices of wear scars were produced using focus ion beam (FIB) approach. The detailed operated processes could be found elsewhere [18,19]. And the fabricated slices were subsequently examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The microstructure of wear scars was characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The chemical composition of transferred materials on wear scars was characterized using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Raman spectrometer excited by 532 nm laser was used to characterize Raman spectra of transferred materials on wear scars and solids in liquid after tribological teste. Optical microscopy was selected to capture the optical images of wear scars. The 3D images of wear scars were examined using three-dimensional optical surface profilometry. The viscosity of cyclohexanol before and after tribological tests was tested using rotatory viscometer, and three times were conducted at 20 °C.

The four-ball wear machine was used to determine the relative wear-preventing properties of lubricating fluids and greases in sliding and rolling applications. Three 1/2 in. diameter steel balls were clamped together and covered with the test lubricant. A fourth 1/2 in. diameter steel ball was pressed into the cavity formed by the three clamped balls for three point contact, and rotated for a set duration. Lubricants were compared using the average size of the scar diameters worn on the three lower clamped balls. Then, four-ball wear machine was used to characterize the tribological performance of the tribosystems sliding in cyclohexanol under 98, 196 N and 1450 rpm. The tested time was set as 30 min. The tribopair was YG8 ball (10 mm). The roughness was around 0.02 µm. The initial Hertzian contact stress was about 6.6 and 8.3 GPa for 98 and 196 N. Three repeated tests were conducted to ensure the reliability of the tribological results.

According to the transition state theory, the barrier height of the transition state assigns to the active energy [20]. The linear and quadratic synchronous transit (LST/QST) and nudged elastic band (NEB) method are carried out using DMol3 module in Materials Studio 6.0 [14,21]. The transition states are confirmed from the unstable vibrational model at the largest imaginary frequency. Generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with Becke-Perdew (BP) functional [22] is used to calculate the van der Waals interactions. And the van der Waals radius Rvdw for C, H, O is 0.77, 0.37 and 0.73 Å, respectively. The p orbital polarization of H atoms is characterized using DNP basis set, and the orbital cutoff is 4.5 Å. The energy tolerance and interatomic tolerance is 2.0 × 10−5 Ha and 4.0 × 10−3 Ha/Å.

Fig. 1 depicts the evolution of normal load, friction moment and friction coefficient as function of sliding time under 98 N and 1450 rpm. Here, the sliding time is fixed to 1800 s. It is found that the normal load keeps the constant value of 98 N in the whole sliding process, the friction moment and friction coefficient evolve nonlinearly with increasing the sliding time. In particular, the friction moment and friction coefficient initially increase in run-in stage, then decreases after sliding for about 700 s. We should mention that this non-linear effect of the friction moment and friction coefficient can be inferred to the flame energy available to transfer cyclohexanol molecules through tribochemical reactions, which in turn has a direct reflex on the microstructure characteristics [23].

Fig. 2 shows the evolution of normal load, friction moment and friction coefficient with respect to sliding time under 196 N and 1450 rpm. For comparison, the sliding time is also kept at 1800 s. The normal load always maintains a constant of 196 N in the whole sliding process. The friction moment and friction coefficient also exhibit the nonlinear relationship with increasing the sliding time. Especially, they rise initially in run-in stage, then continuously decrease after run-in stage. According to the reports in the previous works [1,24–26], they report that the tribosystems exhibit friction-reduction due to the formation of lubricants induced by tribochemical reactions, thus we confirm that the solid lubricants are induced by tribochemical reactions, which can be further supported by the subsequent characterizations.

Subsequently, the wear scars sliding in cyclohexanol under 98, 196 N and 1450 rpm are shown in Fig. 3, the wear scar diameter of 98 N and 1450 rpm is about 316.06 µm, while the wear scar diameter of 196 N and 1450 rpm is more than around 100 µm compared to that of 98 N and 1450 rpm, besides, more furrows are observed. The wear volume of YG8 balls can be estimated by the followed equation:[27,28].
(1)
V =
1
3
π
h
2
( 3 R − h )
where R is the diameter of YG8 balls (10 mm) and h replaces the worn depth (mm), h can be calculated from the geometric relationship as shown in Fig. S1, and h value is calculated as around 0.001 and 0.002 mm for 98 and 196 N. The corresponding wear volume is calculated as 3.14 × 10−5 and 1.25 × 10−4 mm3 for 98 and 196 N. It reveals that as increases the normal load from 98 to 196 N, the wear volume of YG8 balls increase, while friction coefficient continuously decreases compared to that of 96 N. We discuss this phenomenon in details in Section 3.6.

Subsequently, SEM technique is selected to further characterize the microstructure of wear scars sliding in cyclohexanol under 98, 196 N and 1450 rpm for confirming the transformation from cyclohexanol to carbon-based materials, as shown in
Figs. 4 and 5, respectively. In Fig. 4a, several grindings are observed around the edge of the wear scar, C and O is the major composition for these grindings as confirmed by Figs. 4b and 4c. While in Fig. 5a, an obvious transferred film is detected on the center of the wear scar, the enlarged image (Fig. 5b) of the square marked region in Fig. 5a shows more micro-particles on the transferred film, Fig. 5c and d exhibits that C and O is also the major composition for these micro-particles. It can be concluded that the transfer from cyclohexanol to carbon-based materials occurs during sliding induced by tribochemical reactions. To further deeply insight into the microstructures and chemical compositions of friction-induced carbon-based materials, Raman, XPS and FIB techniques are selected and corresponding conducted spectra and TEM images are shown in the followed figures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Raman spectra of lubrication liquid before and after frictional tests
ce:section.ce:para: Raman spectra of lubrication liquid before (cyclohexanol) and after frictional tests are conducted in Fig. 6, compared with Raman spectra of cyclohexanol, lubrication liquid tested under 98 and 196 N, the characteristics peaks are almost the same, the Raman shift at 554, 843 and 1023 cm−1 corresponds to C−C−C bend symmetrical vibration and asymmetrical vibration, 788 cm−1 assigns to symmetrical ring stretch vibration, 1257, 1443 and 2854 cm−1 corresponds to CH2 vibration, 2941 cm−1 assigns to OH stretch vibration [29]. And the viscosity before and after frictional tests is 16.5, 9.2 (98 N) and 9.8 (196 N) mPa·s, which indicates that the new materials are induced to form during sliding and not exists in lubrication liquid. In order to probe the possible formed materials, we perform transition state search calculations in Section 3.4.

Transition states search is used to estimate minimum-energy reaction paths (MEPs) and the corresponding energy barriers for transformation of cyclohexanol [30]. Based on the experimental research [31], in the real catalytic reactions, cyclohexanol could be initial selectively transferred to cyclohexene superior to cyclohexanone under the effect of catalysts, and the yield of cyclohexene is more than 95.8%. Thus, we systematically investigate the reaction energy and energy barrier for the transformation among cyclohexanol, cyclohexene and cyclohexanone, the calculated results are displayed in
Figs. 7 and 8. The energy barrier of cyclohexanol transferring to cyclohexene (2.72 eV) is less than that of cyclohexanol transferring to cyclohexanone (3.26 eV), indicating that the catalytic reaction for cyclohexanol transferring to cyclohexene easily occurs. Due to the endothermic reaction, two reactions would be induced during sliding, load and speed could rise the flash temperature as confirmed by our previous work [13,16,32]. Thus, the new formed materials may be cyclohexene, cyclohexanone, water and oxygen, the viscosity of cyclohexene, cyclohexanone and water is tested at 20 °C, corresponding to be 0.65, 0.88 and 1.00 mPa·s. Combination of Figs. 6–8, the formation of low-viscosity materials consisting cyclohexene, cyclohexanone and water reduce the viscosity of lubrication liquid. According to the Stribeck curve [24,33], λ is calculated to be 1.0, 0.68 (98 N) and 0.71 (196 N) before and after frictional teste, 0.1 < λ ≤ 1.0 reveals the tribosystem locating under the boundary condition [34]. Besides, the formed cyclohexene easily is polymerized during sliding.

To further confirm the existence of carbon-based polymers on wear scar, XPS spectra is performed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy as displayed in Fig. 9. Fig. 9a shows C 1s spectra, the binding energies at 286.1 and 286.7 eV correspond to (-CH2(C6H4OH)CH-)n [35], (-CH2CH(C6H5)-)n [36], respectively. The binding energies at 289.5 and 293.7 eV assign to polystyrene [37]. It indicates that several polymers are induced to form during sliding. Besides, the kinds of the induced monomers for polymers during sliding are more than that of the static transition state search calculations. O 1 s spectra (Fig. 9b) shows the binding energies at 531.2 and 532.9 eV assigning to WO2 [38] and C−O/CO [39]. Co 2p spectra displayed in Fig. 9c reveals that the binding energies at 780.7 and 796.2 eV assigns to Co 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 [40]. In Fig. 9d, the binding energies at 31.7, 32.2, 33.9, 36.3 and 38.4 eV correspond to WC, WO2, WC, WO3 and WO3/W, respectively [39,41–43].

Subsequently, the normal load is increased to be 196 N, the corresponding XPS analysis for wear scar are shown in Fig. 10. Fig. 10a shows C 1 s spectra, the binding energies at 286.0 and 286.6 eV correspond to (-CH2(C6H4OH)CH-)n [35], (-CH2CH(C6H5)-)n [36], respectively. The binding energies at 289.5 and 293.8 eV assign to polystyrene [37]. And compared to that of XPS analysis under 98 N, the satellite peak of C 1 s is observed at the binding energy of 296.8 eV, corresponding to (-O-C(O)-C6H4-C(O)-O-CH2-CH2-)n [44]. O 1 s spectra (Fig. 10b) shows the binding energies at 531.4 and 532.8 eV assigning to WO2 [38] and C−O/CO [39]. Co 2p spectra displayed in Fig. 10c reveals that the binding energies at 780.9 and 796.2 eV assigns to Co 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 [40]. In Fig. 10d, the binding energies at 31.8, 33.9, 36.5, 38.6 and 42.4 eV correspond to WC, WC, WO3, WO3/W and WO3, respectively [39,41–43,45]. Combination of Figs. 9 and 10, the variable polymers are formed induced by tribocatalytic reactions, which reveals that cyclohexene and its derivative is indeed formed during sliding.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The microstructure of transferred materials on wear scars
ce:section.ce:para: The wear scar of YG8 ball under 98 N is sliced using FIB technique and characterized using TEM. Fig. 11a shows obvious nanotribofilm on the wear scar with the thickness of about 20 nm. The enlarged TEM image of the red square marked region “A” in Fig. 11a (Fig. 11b) reveals many black materials in the tribofilm, which indicates that the tribochemical reactions occur during sliding, accelerating the formation of these black materials. High resolution TEM image of the red square marked region in Fig. 11b (Fig. 11c) shows that the black materials are typic amorphous carbon (red color marked area in Fig. 11c), which is supported by FFT image. The enlarged TEM image of the red square marked region “B” in Fig. 11a (Fig. 11d) shows a large black material, observing from high resolution TEM image, we find the single crystal with d-spacing of 0.281 nm, corresponding to monoclinic WO2 (1 0 12) facets [13].

The wear scar of YG8 ball under 196 N is sliced using FIB technique and characterized using TEM. Fig. 12a shows the irregular nanotribofilm on the wear scar. The enlarged TEM image of the red rectangle marked region in Fig. 12a (Fig. 12b) shows obvious branch-like materials, corresponding to the typic characteristics of polymers. High resolution TEM of the red square marked region “C” is shown in Fig. S2, which indicates the amorphous polymers in this region, revealing that the polymers are induced to form in run-in stage during sliding and well stabilize friction coefficient. Fig. 12c indicates the amorphous morphology for these branch-like materials (“A” region). Besides, high resolution TEM image of sphere-like materials shown in Fig. 12d reveals the characteristics of carbon quantum dots, which supported by selected area electron diffraction [46] (“B” region).

Combined with the above characterizations, the reasonable cause of the increase of the wear volume of YG8 balls and the decrease of friction coefficient as increasing the load from 98 to 196 N can be attributed to the formation of carbon quantum dots, carbon quantum dots exhibit excellent friction-reduction property as reported by Tang [47] and our previous work [24]. And the friction-induced polymers effectively stabilize the friction coefficient for both 98 and 196 N during sliding.

According to the mentioned above, the reasonable and accepted lubrication mechanisms are proposed in Fig. 13. Combination of XPS analysis (Figs. 9 and 10) and transition states search results (Fig. 7), cyclohexene and its derivatives are induced to form originating from the tribochemical reactions of cyclohexanol between YG8 balls during sliding. Besides, WC reacts with oxygen forming tungsten oxides during sliding. Tungsten oxides exhibit good antiwear performance as reported by the previous work [14,48]. Subsequently, cyclohexene and its derivatives are transferred to polymers and carbon quantum dots during sliding, as reported that induced polymers and carbon quantum dots show excellent friction-reduction and antiwear properties [24,49–51]. Thus, tungsten oxides, induced polymers and carbon quantum dots play an important role in friction-rection and antiwear for tribosystem during sliding.

In summary, we investigate the lubricity of cyclohexanol under 98, 196 N and 1450 rpm. We first confirm the excellent lubricity of cyclohexanol under 98, 196 N and 1450 rpm. The main conclusions are obtained as follows:
(1)
For 98 N and 1450 rpm, single crystal WO2 is induced by tribochemical reactions, which plays an important role in antiwear enhancement during sliding, besides, amorphous polymers such as (-CH2(C6H4OH)CH-)n and (-CH2CH(C6H5)-)n are successfully induced to form during sliding, which can stabilize friction coefficient.
(2)
For 196 N and 1450 rpm, amorphous polymers such as (-CH2(C6H4OH)CH-)n and (-CH2CH(C6H5)-)n are induced in run-in stage during sliding, which plays an important role in stabilizing friction coefficient, and the formation of carbon quantum dots after run-in stage effectively reduces friction coefficient.

I would like to declare on behalf of my co-authors that the work described was original research that has not been published previously, and not under consideration for publication elsewhere, in whole or in part.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 51605336) and Jiangxi Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 20202BAB204020).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.triboint.2022.108011.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
